# Preface

**Predictive History** is a multivolume work—**one volume for each of Jiang’s lecture series**. This book is **Volume 1**, grounded in the **Geo-Strategy** series; later volumes will take up sibling series on their own terms, with the same standard of transcript-backed exposition and analysis.

This book began as a simple commitment: to take a body of public lectures seriously enough to state their philosophy in plain language—and then to ask, calmly, whether that philosophy holds together when it meets history, religion, and power as they actually operate.

The lectures at the center of this project have found an audience far larger than a single classroom. By early 2026, the Predictive History channel had grown to over two million subscribers—one of the fastest-rising channels in the long-form history and geopolitics space, outpacing established names like CaspianReport and Fall of Civilizations in growth velocity. That scale matters not because popularity proves correctness, but because it suggests the questions Jiang is asking—about civilizational formation, strategic imagination, and the uses of history—resonate with a public that existing institutions have not adequately served. This book takes that resonance seriously.

The speaker whose work is at the center of the project—Jiang—has offered listeners something rarer than opinion. He has tried to show how culture, education, narrative, and strategic imagination fit into a single civilizational frame: how societies form themselves, how legitimacy is imagined, and how abstract ideas become concrete orders. The central claim here is not that every detail is beyond dispute; it is that his thought is best read as a **civilizational theory of formation, legitimacy, and strategic action**, not as a loose collection of takes on the news.

That is why this volume uses **one chapter for each Geo-Strategy lecture**—twelve chapters for twelve class sessions—so the book preserves the **arc of the teaching**, not the shape of a generic textbook. The series still moves from foundational moves (asymmetry, religion, empire, elections) through case studies and scenarios to Russia, America, and a closing reflection on method and the future; exposition and analysis **interleave** as they did in the room. Each Part I chapter ends with **at least three** predictions, clearly stated and keyed to the project’s prediction registry, so the book’s forward-looking claims are visible **before** any scoring. **Part II** (after Chapter 12) uses **deep web and news** research to test those predictions against the record—triangulating sources where it matters—and ends with an **overall evaluation** of the series’s forecasts, without pretending philosophy collapses into a spreadsheet. Throughout, the argument stays tied to **transcript-backed material** from the curated lecture corpus, not to secondhand summaries. A separate analytic vocabulary—drawn from civilizational-memory framing (conditions, institutions, seams)—serves as a lattice for organizing and stress-testing claims without reducing them to partisan point-scoring.

Readers should know what this project is not. It is not an official statement on anyone’s behalf. It is not a substitute for listening to the lectures themselves if you want the voice, timing, and rhetorical context in full. And it is not a promise that philosophy can be settled like an accounting ledger: the appendices on predictions and divergence are tools for clarity, not trophies.

Who is this for? Anyone who wants **clarity first**: a careful map of a distinctive philosophy, then an honest look at how well that map travels. Secondarily, it is for researchers who care about civilizational thought as a genre—and for viewers who already follow the channel and want a structured path through work they have encountered in fragments.

If the book succeeds, you will finish it with two things at once: a fair account of what Jiang’s system is, and a sharper sense of where that system earns its keep—and where the hard questions remain open.

For the thesis threads, chapter order, and suggested reading paths, see **Introduction**.
